GateDocument.TextWithNodes: "Why redecorating should start with your floor", "When you spend so much time at home, as most of us currently are, you begin to realise the things about your space that you want to change. And while we don't condone rushing off to the hardware store and taking matters into your own hands, there's certainly no better time to plan your next interior decorating move than this moment of stillness we're currently in. But when it comes to giving your space an actual facelift, knowing where to start is the hardest part. Do you choose the wall colours and then move onto the floors? Do you select your artwork and then build things out from there? Naturally, every interior decorator has their own theory and approach. But according to the experts at Carpet Court (who assure us they're not biased, though they are Australia's largest flooring and blinds specialist) it's actually really simple: start with the floor. Just like shoes set the tone of your outfit, a good floor can make or break the feng shui of a room. When it comes to putting your #housegoals mood board together, then, there are two emerging design trends that you should be paying close attention to – before everyone else on Instagram catches up. Nette King says this return to colour is probably a reaction to the minimalism of the Marie Kondo-fever that swept through design a couple of years ago. But she says it's not about going totally maximalist, either. "It's more refined than that. Think of it as 'curated maximalism'," she instructs. Instead of trying to create a flavour explosion of coloured rugs and mismatched objects, pick one or two jewel-toned colours – whether that's a plush pink carpet or an emerald green upholstery, and compliment that with more subdued accents. Nette King suggests matte-finish ceramics and abstract artworks. The other trend Carpet Court has seen an uptick of interest in is something they're calling 'Raw Luxe' It emphasises a pared-back but earthy colour palette, and textures that are reminiscent of the natural environment, such as oak floorboards or a chunky pile rug. "The idea is to include discreet hints of opulence that elevate natural materials and forms," says Nette King, like brass fixtures and rattan furniture. "The result? Calm, nurturing spaces that draw nature inside your home." Both trends are evocative of this moment in time – where 'colour play' celebrates the role of texture and tone in bringing a sense of spontaneity and liveliness to the home, 'raw luxe' taps into our desire to feel connected to the landscape at a time when going outside has become somewhat of a luxury. Interior stylist and Carpet Court ambassador Heather Nette King calls the first of these trends 'Colour Play' She says it's about pairing unexpected colours and textures together, and "having the confidence to break the so-called 'rules' of decorating and creating spaces that bring you joy." Of course, redecorating your house based on trends could sound like risky business, especially when most of us can only afford to mix things up every few years. But these are better thought of as concepts. And, given they encourage thoughtful flourishes of colour and texture rather than total commitment to a specific theme, we expect them to age remarkably well. So, if you've been thinking about pulling the trigger on transforming your space, now is the time to get planning. Just remember: it all starts with the floor."
